one_line_takeaway: GateScope is a black-box auditing framework that fingerprints LLM identity via structured JSON-schema probes plus per-model XGBoost classifiers (avg F1 0.968 on 24 vendor models), and applying it to 10 real commercial LLM gateways finds gateways with response-identification rates as low as 13-48% for a claimed model, multi-turn context/fingerprint instability, billing overcharges up to +62.8% relative to published rates, and up to ~2x elevated latency variance -- exposing largely undisclosed opacity in third-party LLM gateway behavior.
key_finding: Controlled validation achieved an average F1 of 0.968 +/- 0.085 across the 24 official-API models (most above 0.95) for distinguishing model identity from response-content signatures, including closely related family variants (e.g., gpt-4o-mini vs. gpt-4.1-nano), and correctly rejected all 5 unseen models as not matching any known class. Applied to the 10 gateways, response-content classification (Table 1, 275 responses/model) showed the baseline vendor APIs at 96-98% self-identification, while several gateways fell well below this: e.g., for gpt-5 responses, b*ie was identified as the claimed model only 13.09% of the time and a*yi only 48.00%, versus a 97.09% baseline -- evidence of likely silent model substitution. Multi-turn results (Table 2) showed the baseline gpt-4o passing all 5/5/5 turn-10/24/25 checkpoints with 1 fingerprint, while several gateways failed checkpoints at turns 24/25 or showed multiple system_fingerprint values (e.g., a*yi showed only 3/1/2 checkpoint passes and 4 distinct fingerprints for gpt-4o, versus baseline's 5/5/5 and 1 fingerprint, with cache rate collapsing to 4.2% vs. baseline's 48.7%); gpt-4o-mini was less stable even at baseline (5/1/2), and platform o*ey showed the inverse anomaly -- higher checkpoint pass rates than baseline (5/4/4) but 0.0% cache usage versus baseline's 40.3%, suggesting a non-canonical implementation. Billing accuracy (Table 3, gpt-4o) showed most gateways matching expected cost exactly (0.0% gap), but a*ix showed a +7.6% gap ($6.07 expected vs. $6.53 charged) and o*ey a +62.8% gap ($6.81 expected vs. $11.09 charged) despite comparable token usage to other platforms. Latency CV (Table 4, gpt-4o) was near-baseline (CV~0.42-0.68) for most gateways but markedly elevated for b*ie (CV 0.82-1.10 across all four probe domains, roughly double the baseline's 0.35-0.67), consistent with unstable/heterogeneous backend routing.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GateScope, a lightweight black-box measurement framework for auditing behavioral consistency and operational transparency of third-party commercial LLM API gateways (proxies that route requests to multiple upstream vendor models such as GPT, Claude, Gemini under a unified API). GateScope audits along four dimensions: (1) response content analysis, which fingerprints models using a structured probe suite (AIME math, GPQA, factual recall on 2021-2025 events, and geographic multi-hop inference) that forces each response into a JSON schema with 'knowledge_path' and 'final_answer' fields, extracts a 5-family behavioral signature vector (answer quality, reasoning structure, scale, style, parsing quality), and trains a per-model one-vs-rest XGBoost classifier to detect silent model substitution; (2) multi-turn conversation performance, using a purpose-built 25-turn conversation template with an identity/preference checkpoint at turns 10, 24, 25, tracking Memory Checkpoint Pass Rate, System Fingerprint Stability (count of distinct system_fingerprint values), and Cached Token Usage Rate to detect silent truncation or mid-conversation model switching; (3) billing accuracy, comparing an analytically expected cost C_expected = (n_in - n_cached)*p_in + n_cached*p_cached + n_out*p_out against the gateway-reported charge; and (4) latency characteristics, using coefficient of variation (CV = sigma_T/mu_T) over repeated same-probe requests as a supporting (not conclusive) signal of backend heterogeneity. The authors validate the response-content fingerprinting on 24 official vendor model endpoints before applying GateScope, unmodified, to 10 anonymized real-world commercial LLM gateways.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attacker threat model but a client-side trust/transparency auditing model: the LLM gateway operator is the (potentially) misbehaving party, and the auditor is an ordinary API client with no privileged access, using only the public OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint. The concerns audited are gateway-side misbehaviors that would harm or deceive paying users: silently substituting a cheaper/weaker model than the one requested (undisclosed downgrading), silently truncating prompt history or output to save tokens/cost, misreporting token usage or over-billing relative to published prices, and unstable/inconsistent latency that may indicate undisclosed backend switching. There is no discussion of external attackers, jailbreaks, prompt injection, or model-security vulnerabilities in the conventional adversarial-ML sense -- the 'security and privacy' framing in the paper's own CCS classification concerns software/service-level integrity and billing fraud of an opaque intermediary, not model robustness or exploitation.
limitations: The paper explicitly frames latency as only a 'supporting signal' since it 'cannot be determined from latency measurements alone' whether variance stems from model switching, gateway load, or network conditions. Measurements were taken from a single fixed vantage point at a single point in time, so results are explicitly stated as 'a point-in-time snapshot rather than a long-term ranking.' Billing-gap analysis relies on the gateway's own self-reported token counts rather than independently verified ground-truth token counts, so it detects inconsistency between a gateway's reported usage and its published price, not necessarily fraud in the token count itself. Response-content classifiers are validated per-model in a closed-set (one-vs-rest) fashion; the paper does not report false-positive substitution-detection rates under legitimate but out-of-suite prompt distributions in the gateway setting itself (only the controlled 24+5 model setting). Gateway identities are anonymized by the authors, limiting reproducibility/attribution of findings to specific named commercial services. The authors state future work is needed to extend to continuous, multi-vantage-point monitoring and to better attribute root causes of observed inconsistencies.

datasets_used: 24 official LLMs queried directly via vendor APIs (controlled validation set, 15,840 records, 660 per model, K=12 repetitions per probe)
5 additional unseen LLMs for generalization testing (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Haiku 4.5, Qwen-plus, Qwen-turbo, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp)
10 anonymized commercial LLM API gateways (a*yi, a*ix, b*ie, l*ub, o*ub, o*er, o*ey, un*pi, ui*pi, z*ng)
Custom 4-domain probe suite: AIME math problems, GPQA multiple-choice, Factual Recall (2021-2025 events), Geographic Information Inference (built on discrete concept composition)
Custom 25-turn multi-turn conversation template with identity/preference checkpoints at turns 10, 24, 25
quantitative_results: Average F1 = 0.968 +/- 0.085 across 24 vendor models for model-identity classification; most models >0.95
0 of 5 unseen models misclassified as any known model (conservative generalization)
Response-content identification of claimed model: baseline vendor APIs 96-98%; worst gateways as low as 13.09% (b*ie, gpt-5) and 48.00% (a*yi, gpt-5)
Multi-turn checkpoint pass rate (gpt-4o, turns 10/24/25): baseline 5/5/5 with 1 fingerprint and 48.7% cache rate; a*yi 3/1/2 with 4 fingerprints and 4.2% cache rate
Billing gap (gpt-4o): baseline, most gateways 0.0% gap; a*ix +7.6% ($6.07 expected vs $6.53 actual); o*ey +62.8% ($6.81 expected vs $11.09 actual)
Latency CV (gpt-4o, across Math/GPQA/Factual/Geo probes): baseline 0.35-0.67; b*ie 0.82-1.10 (worst, roughly 2x baseline)
baselines_compared: Official vendor model API endpoints (used as the ground-truth/baseline behavior for response content, multi-turn consistency, billing, and latency comparisons)

Prior LLM fingerprinting methods referenced as related but not re-implemented as experimental baselines: LLMmap (active probing for open-source models), instructional fingerprints, UTF, AuditLLM, FDLLM -- the paper notes these focus on model-trait/artifact identification rather than third-party gateway transparency/billing compliance
